title: Different Ways to Evaluate Boolean Expression

statement.markdown:
Given a boolean expression string `expression` containing the characters '0', '1', '(', ')', 'NOT', 'AND', and 'OR', evaluate the expression and return all possible results. The expression can be evaluated in different ways due to the presence of boolean operators.

Return a list of all possible boolean results of evaluating the expression.

constraints.description: The length of the expression string.
The expression string can only contain the characters '0', '1', '(', ')', 'NOT', 'AND', and 'OR'.

examples.explanation: The expression can be evaluated in two ways: ((0 AND 1) AND (1)) = 0 and ((0 AND 1) AND (1 OR 1)) = 0.
The expression can be evaluated in two ways: ((0 OR 1) AND (1)) = 1 and ((0 OR 1) AND (1 OR 1)) = 1.

hints.text: The problem involves a boolean expression with operators 'NOT', 'AND', 'OR', and operands '0', '1'. The expression can be evaluated in multiple ways due to the presence of these operators.
The expression string can contain subexpressions enclosed in parentheses. These subexpressions can be evaluated independently and their results can be used to evaluate the overall expression.
The problem can be solved using recursion and/or dynamic programming. The key insight is to consider all possible ways to evaluate subexpressions and combine the results using the operators.
Memoization can be used to optimize the solution by storing the results of subexpressions that have already been evaluated. This can help avoid redundant computations.

The core technique/pattern to solve this problem is to use a recursive approach with memoization, where each subexpression is evaluated independently and its results are combined using the operators. The solution involves parsing the expression, identifying subexpressions, and evaluating them recursively.

editorial.title: Different Ways to Evaluate Boolean Expression
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding all possible ways to evaluate a boolean expression. A brute force approach would involve recursively trying all possible combinations of boolean values for the subexpressions. However, this approach can be optimized by observing that the result of a subexpression only depends on the operator and the results of its subexpressions.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve recursively trying all possible combinations of boolean values for the subexpressions. This approach would be correct but slow, with an exponential time complexity due to the repeated computation of subexpressions.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to use dynamic programming to store the results of subexpressions and avoid repeated computation. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the result of a subexpression only depends on the operator and the results of its subexpressions.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a recursive approach with memoization to store the results of subexpressions. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the length of the expression string.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proved by induction on the structure of the expression. The base case is when the expression is a single boolean value, in which case the result is trivial. For the inductive step, we assume that the result is correct for subexpressions and show that it is correct for the entire expression. This involves showing that the result of a subexpression only depends on the operator and the results of its subexpressions.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution on a sample input reveals that it produces the correct results. For example, on the input '((0 AND 1) OR 1)', the solution produces the correct results [True, True].
commonMistakes: Not handling the base cases correctly
Not storing the results of subexpressions to avoid repeated computation
Not handling the different operators (AND, OR, NOT) correctly
Not parsing the expression string correctly
Not handling the recursive structure of the expression correctly
Not using memoization to optimize the solution
